title: Harcha
subtitle: Moroccan semolina pan bread with a sandy crust
excerpt: Harcha is a quick Moroccan pan bread made primarily from fine semolina, with butter and milk to bind. No yeast, no rising time: it goes from mixing to table in under 30 minutes. The crust is lightly crisped in a dry pan while the interior stays soft and buttery. It is traditionally eaten at breakfast with honey or jam.
sourceNotes: Harcha is a standard part of the Moroccan breakfast table alongside msemen and baghrir. It is documented in Paula Wolfert's 'Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco' (1973) and in Nargisse Benkabbou's 'Casablanca: My Moroccan Food' (2018). The name refers to its rough, sandy texture, which comes from the coarse semolina.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. Leftovers can be reheated in a dry pan over low heat for 2 minutes per side.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: pan-fried over low-medium heat; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: Fine semolina is the ground inner of the durum wheat grain with most of the bran removed. It is finer than coarse polenta but coarser than plain white flour, and it absorbs liquid more slowly. In harcha, this slower absorption gives the bread its characteristic dense, slightly grainy interior and the sandy crust that forms in a dry pan. Using coarse semolina instead of fine produces a grittier result; using plain flour alone removes the texture entirely.

content.text: The character of harcha comes from
: the slow-absorbing grain that gives the crust its sandy bite and the crumb its dense, butter-rich interior. Use fine semolina rather than coarse for a more uniform texture.

The dough should come together as a soft, slightly crumbly mass that can be pressed into rounds without cracking at the edges. If it crumbles, add milk a teaspoon at a time. Do not overwork it once it comes together.

Combine the 250 g semolina, 50 g plain flour, 1 tsp baking powder and 5 g salt in a bowl. Pour in the 60 g melted butter and rub through with your fingertips until the mixture resembles damp sand. Add the 120 ml warm milk and mix until a soft, cohesive dough forms. It should hold together when pressed but not be sticky.
Divide the dough into 4 to 6 pieces. Press each into a round about 1.5 cm thick and 10 cm across, using your palm. Dust lightly with fine semolina on both sides.

Heat a dry heavy pan over low-medium heat. Cook the harcha for 8 to 10 minutes on the first side until golden brown with a slightly darker edge, then flip and cook for a further 8 to 10 minutes until the second side is golden and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The crust should feel firm when pressed. Serve warm.

items.solution: The dough needs more milk. Add a teaspoon at a time and work it in briefly until the dough holds together cleanly without cracking at the edges. Semolina takes a moment to fully absorb liquid, so pause between additions.

items.problem: Raw, doughy centre after the crust has coloured
items.solution: The heat was too high. Harcha needs a slow, gentle cook to allow the heat to penetrate. Reduce to low heat, cover the pan loosely with a lid to trap steam, and add 3 to 4 minutes to each side.
The dough was overworked after adding the milk, which develops the small amount of gluten in the semolina. Mix only until the dough comes together and then stop.
